_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Variables
summary: Give values clear names and decide whether they may change.
refresher: A C++ variable starts with a type, followed by its name and an optional value. For example, `int score = 10;` stores a whole number. Add `const` when the value should not be reassigned.

blocks.body: A variable is a named place to keep a value. In C++, you write the type before the name: `int attempts = 0;`. The type tells C++ what kind of value belongs there, and the semicolon ends the declaration.

A variable keeps the same type while it exists. A name declared inside braces can be used only inside that block. Give local variables a starting value before reading them so the program never has to guess what they contain.

blocks.title: Python chooses the type for you
blocks.body: Python lets you write `score = 10`. C++ writes the type as part of the declaration: `int score = 10;`. That extra word lets the compiler catch assignments that do not match.

blocks.caption: The course name stays fixed because it is const. The enrolled count may change, so the program adds one before printing.
Initialize a local variable before any branch can read it.

blocks.body: If `ready` is false in the first version, `result` never receives a value. Starting it at zero gives the program a clear fallback. Use `const` for a value such as a course name or conversion rate that should stay fixed.

blocks.title: Initialize before use
blocks.body: A local number without a starting value does not contain a dependable answer. Assign a value when you declare it, unless every possible path clearly assigns one before it is read.

blocks.prompt: Which declaration creates a changing integer?
blocks.why: A plain int variable can be reassigned after its initialized declaration.
Compound assignment adds three to the numeric value and stores seven.

blocks.title: Variables checklist
items: Keep scope as narrow as practical.
